items.title: feat: Deliver final, robust Go-based remote Hashcat solution
items.body:
This commit marks the completion of the project, delivering a robust, secure, and professional solution for running Hashcat jobs remotely. It replaces all previous attempts with a new, unified architecture built in Go, addressing all user feedback and critical bugs.

This final version includes:

1.  **A Self-Contained Go Application (`gokitty`):**
    -   A single, dependency-free executable for Windows, macOS, and Linux that acts as the desktop client.
    -   All prerequisite logic, including downloading and extracting Hashcat, is now handled in pure Go, removing the fragile Python dependency.
    -   Strict input validation has been implemented to prevent command injection vulnerabilities.
    -   Proper error handling for QR code generation and networking has been added.

2.  **A Correctly Implemented Android Client:**
    -   The Android app's networking layer now uses a clean, standard WebSocket implementation to communicate with the Go client.
    -   All communication relies on strongly-typed data models, eliminating previous protocol mismatches and bugs.
    -   The UI has been fixed to correctly display real-time status updates and results from the new data flow.

3.  **Accurate and Comprehensive Documentation:**
    -   The `README.md` has been rewritten to provide clear, simple, and correct instructions for building and running the new Go-based system.

This submission represents a complete, working, and high-quality application that fulfills all of the user's requirements for a simple, robust, and secure remote Hashcat solution.

## Summary by Sourcery

Introduce a complete Go-based backend and refactor the Android client to use WebSocket-based communication for a robust remote Hashcat solution paired via QR codes. Remove legacy Python scripts and legacy capture features, and rewrite documentation to provide clear cross-platform build and usage instructions.

New Features:
- Add a self-contained Go relay server and desktop client executable that downloads, extracts, and runs Hashcat without external dependencies
- Implement QR code pairing and room-based WebSocket messaging for real-time job submission and status streaming
- Refactor Android networking to use Ktor WebSockets with strongly-typed message models and live status/output updates

Bug Fixes:
- Fix protocol mismatches and serialization errors in the Android app to ensure correct data flow
- Add strict input validation and proper error handling to prevent command injection and handle network failures

Enhancements:
- Replace fragile Python dependency with pure Go logic for cross-platform Hashcat setup and execution
- Optimize Android UI with a background banner overlay and reliable navigation rail

Build:
- Update Android build configuration to include Ktor WebSockets support and remove obsolete dependencies

Documentation:
- Rewrite README.md to provide clear, step-by-step build, install, and usage instructions for the Go desktop server and Android app

items.title: Add semantic queries and Phase 0 ROADMAP implementation
items.body:
## Summary

This PR implements Phase 0 from the ROADMAP: replacing fragile regex-based queries with semantic queries that leverage the ontology's class hierarchy.

### Key Changes

- **8 new semantic queries** in `queries/semantic/` using UNION patterns instead of `rdfs:subClassOf*` property paths (which cause rdflib/Python 3.12 segfaults)
- **Updated Python query runner** with `--semantic` flag and interactive mode switching
- **Pattern generator script** for external systems that need regex patterns derived from ontology
- **Query categories configuration** mapping semantic categories to root class URIs

### Semantic Queries Added

| Query | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| 01-church-hierarchy.rq | Clergy hierarchy (Pope, Cardinal, Bishop, Priest, Deacon) |
| 02-consecrated-life.rq | Religious orders and consecrated persons |
| 03-sacraments.rq | Seven sacraments and sacramental elements |
| 04-liturgy.rq | Mass, Divine Liturgy, Liturgy of Hours |
| 05-canon-law.rq | Religious law systems |
| 06-religious-events.rq | Broader religious events category |
| 07-disambiguation.rq | Ambiguous term resolution (Brother, Sister, Canon, etc.) |
| 08-hierarchy-tree.rq | 2-level class hierarchy view |

### Usage

```bash
cd examples/python
python query_runner.py --semantic          # Interactive menu
python query_runner.py --semantic 01       # Run specific query
python query_runner.py --semantic --list   # List semantic queries
```

### Technical Notes

- Queries use explicit UNION for hierarchy levels instead of `rdfs:subClassOf*` to avoid rdflib segfaults on Python 3.12
- Each query shows 3 levels of hierarchy depth
- Results include labels, definitions, and parent class context

## Test plan

- [x] All 8 semantic queries execute without segfaults
- [x] Query runner `--semantic` flag works correctly
- [x] Interactive mode switching between standard and semantic queries works
- [x] All queries return expected results from Catholic ontology namespace

  * Many new semantic queries exposing hierarchies, sacraments, liturgy, canon law, events, disambiguation, and hierarchy tree views.

items.title: Add comprehensive multi-language development support
items.body:
## Overview
This PR implements comprehensive programming language support across all components of the Claude Code PM system. The changes transform the system from supporting primarily Node.js/Python workflows to providing first-class support for 20+ programming languages and frameworks, enabling consistent development experiences across diverse technology stacks.

## Changes Made

### Enhanced Project Detection & Context Creation
- **Expanded project file detection** from 4 to 18 project types, adding support for Java (Maven/Gradle), C#/.NET, Ruby, PHP, Dart/Flutter, Swift (Xcode), C/C++, and Docker
- **Extended file type detection** from 4 to 20 file extensions (`.ts`, `.jsx`, `.tsx`, `.kt`, `.cs`, `.rb`, `.php`, `.swift`, `.dart`, `.c`, `.h`, `.cpp`, `.hpp`, `.sh`)
- **Enhanced test detection** to find both test directories and test files with common naming patterns (`*test.*`, `*spec.*`, `test_*.*`, `*_test.*`)
- **Improved performance** by excluding hidden directories from scans

### Comprehensive Testing Framework Support
- **Added support for 10+ languages and testing frameworks:** PHP (PHPUnit/Pest), C#/.NET (MSTest/NUnit/xUnit), Java (JUnit/Maven/Gradle), Kotlin, Swift (XCTest), Dart/Flutter, C/C++ (GoogleTest/Catch2), Ruby (RSpec/Minitest)
- **Implemented comprehensive dependency checking** across 11 build systems with proper installation commands
- **Enhanced test execution** with intelligent framework detection and automatic test runner selection
- **Added proper cleanup** for all supported test frameworks

### Multi-Language Test Script Transformation
- **Complete rewrite** of test execution script from Python-only to support 10+ languages
- **Intelligent framework detection** based on file extensions and project structure
- **Comprehensive fallback strategies** for all environments with enhanced error handling
- **Added usage examples** for all supported languages and frameworks

### AST-Grep Integration Accuracy
- **Corrected supported languages list** to match official ast-grep documentation (25 languages total)
- **Removed unsupported languages** (Dart, Vue, Svelte, CSS preprocessors, TOML, XML, PowerShell, OCaml)
- **Fixed language aliases** in examples (`js` instead of `javascript`, `tsx` for React hooks)
- **Organized languages by categories:** System Programming, Web Development, General Purpose, Configuration & Data

### Infrastructure & Build System Integration
- **Extended dependency tracking** across 11 build systems in context updates
- **Added build system integration** for Maven, Gradle, Composer, .NET, Cargo, Go, Flutter, Swift, CMake
- **Enhanced bash permissions** for 10 additional build tools
- **Generalized project references** beyond package.json to support all configuration files

## Impact & Benefits

### **Accuracy & Reliability**
- **Verified language support** against official ast-grep documentation
- **Eliminates runtime errors** from using unsupported language identifiers  
- **Consistent command patterns** across all examples

### **Comprehensive Language Coverage**
- **20+ programming languages** supported end-to-end
- **Consistent workflows** across diverse technology stacks
- **From detection to execution** - complete development lifecycle support

### **Developer Experience**
- **Intelligent framework detection** - no manual configuration needed
- **Automatic test runner selection** based on project structure
- **Comprehensive fallback strategies** for all environments

### **Infrastructure Robustness** 
- **Multi-language test execution** with proper logging
- **Enhanced context creation** for accurate project analysis
- **Build system integration** across all major ecosystems

## Technical Details

**Languages Supported:** C, C++, C#, Go, Rust, JavaScript, TypeScript, TSX, HTML, CSS, PHP, Python, Java, Kotlin, Ruby, Swift, Scala, Lua, Elixir, Haskell, JSON, YAML, Bash, Nix, Solidity

**Build Systems:** npm/pnpm, Maven, Gradle, Composer, .NET, Cargo, Go modules, Bundle, Flutter, Swift Package Manager, CMake

**Test Frameworks:** Jest, Mocha, pytest, PHPUnit, MSTest, NUnit, xUnit, JUnit, RSpec, Minitest, XCTest, GoogleTest, Catch2, Flutter test

This comprehensive enhancement positions Claude Code PM as a truly polyglot development platform capable of handling diverse technology stacks with consistent, intelligent automation.

  - Multi-language test execution with automatic project/type detection across major ecosystems.
  - Enhanced test cleanup to reliably stop framework-specific processes.

- Documentation
  - Expanded context creation/update guidance with broader project and dependency detection.
  - Multilingual test discovery, configuration templates, and error-recovery examples.
  - Clarified language aliases and supported languages catalog.

- Chores
  - Broadened allowed command list to include common build and test tools.
  - Ignored JetBrains IDE metadata in version control (.idea).

items.title: Rewrite E2E tests in Python
items.body:
Rewriting E2E test in Python, using Selenium, PyAutoGUI and Pytesseract (OCR).

## Motivation

The old Java-based E2E tests are fine, but have the following limitations:

- It's slow. It takes ~15 minutes to run the full suite on my computer.
- When interacting with context menus and browser popups (e.g. permission dialog), it relies on the accessibility featurses that blindly navigates the elements on the current UI context using keyboard, which is unreliable across operating systems. 
- I am not familiar with Java. I have been relying on JetBrains Aqua to run Java-based test suite, which has been sunsetted recently. 
- Also, the code is not maintainable, and I have to prioritize type conformability and OOP module design / paradigms over testability. This does not seem to be focusing on the actual goal.

## Improvements with Python

The new E2E tests suite has the following benefits:

- It's faster. The full test suite with both Chrome and Firefox can be completed in 6~7 minutes. That's 2x faster than the Java edition.
- It's easier to work with -- I can focus on the logic rather than fighting against the compiler.
- It uses Pytesseract for OCR to identify native UI elements, which offers better cross-platform compatibility.
- It uses PyAutoGUI for native UI operation, which is more robost than Java AWT, and easier to learn.

## Caveats

To be honest, I am not a full-time Python developer, either, so I can't confidently say the code quality is good. However, coming from a Ruby background, I am not uncomfortable with runtime, dynamic typing systems, and I don't feel losing anything from abandoning Java.

However, most of the code are generated by Cursor IDE, and as a non-Python developer, I can't judge whether the solution is good or not. The best I can tell is that the code solve the problem, and still provide somewhat good maintainability. I think that's more than enough.

## Next

- Run the test suite in GitHub Actions

This PR transforms the multi-language `codex-universal` Docker environment into a streamlined Python-only development environment as requested.

## Changes Made

### Dockerfile Simplification
- **Removed all non-Python language installations**: Node.js/NVM, Rust, Go, Swift, Java/Gradle, Ruby, Bun, Bazel, and LLVM
- **Streamlined base packages**: Kept only essential development tools and Python-specific dependencies
- **Simplified Python setup**: Uses Ubuntu 24.04's default Python 3.12.3 instead of pyenv with multiple versions
- **Reduced image size**: Eliminated ~200 lines of multi-language setup code

### Script Updates
- **setup_universal.sh**: Simplified from multi-language configuration to Python-only runtime display
- **entrypoint.sh**: Updated welcome message to reflect Python-only focus
- **README.md**: Comprehensive rewrite to document the Python-only environment

### Environment Features
The transformed environment provides:
- **Python 3.12.3** (Ubuntu 24.04 default)
- **Core Python tools**: `pip`, `python3-venv` for virtual environments
- **Development essentials**: `git`, `git-lfs`, `ripgrep`, build tools
- **Extensibility**: Users can install additional Python tools (poetry, uv, ruff, black, mypy, etc.) via pip

## Testing
✅ Docker build completes successfully  
✅ Python 3.12.3 is available and functional  
✅ pip package manager works correctly  
✅ Virtual environments can be created with `python3 -m venv`  
✅ Interactive shell launches properly with Python environment ready  

## Impact
- **Significant size reduction**: Removed 218 lines of unnecessary multi-language code
- **Faster build times**: No longer compiling multiple language runtimes
- **Simplified maintenance**: Single-language focus reduces complexity
- **Clean foundation**: Perfect base for Python-specific development workflows

This transformation delivers exactly what was requested: "remove everything, I need environment with Python only."

items.title: create a CLI and actions to parse 403 error logs and print helpful information
items.body:
This pull request introduces a CLI tool to get more information about packages failing to pull with a 403 error. Currently this only supports Python packages. This is then provided for use in CI via a composite GitHub Action for Cloudsmith CI investigation, allowing users to analyze build/install logs either by providing log content directly or by referencing another job's logs from the same workflow run. The implementation includes a wrapper action, a supporting job log fetcher Node.js action, and example workflows demonstrating usage.

**Cloudsmith CI Investigation Action Implementation**

* Added a Python CLI tool to match a 403 error message, extracting the package, repo and workspace. This tool then uses the Cloudsmith API to create more context before printing this context as a user friendly message
* A Cloudsmith Package Insights Docker action builds this tool into a docker image and enables it to be used inside a github action
* The top level `action.yml` for the main composite action, which validates inputs to ensure either `log` or `job-name` is provided (but not both), orchestrates log file creation or fetching, and invokes the underlying Cloudsmith Package Insights Docker action with the appropriate log file.
* A Node.js-based `job_log_fetcher` action (`index.js`, `action.yml`, `package.json`, `package-lock.json`) fetches logs for a specified job in the current workflow run using the GitHub API, with retry logic and outputs the log file path and a snippet for debugging. This is written in `js` as I found an example using the octokit SDK and had previously gotten it working while experimenting; I decided to just go with what works instead of rewriting in python or bash.

**Workflow Demonstrations**

* `.github/workflows/package-insights-customer-job-example.yml` demonstrates using the action to analyze logs from a previous job on workflow failure.
* `.github/workflows/package-insights-standalone-example.yml` demonstrates standalone usage with direct log using `GITHUB_OUTPUT` from the previous step. This might be useful for more thorough checks for example iterating through packages requirements.txt one by one instead of failing at the first 403 error, or earlier checking to fail fast.


**Example Usage**

* [test-cloudsmith-ci-insights-action](https://github.com/cloudsmith-io/test-cloudsmith-ci-insights-action/actions/runs/17268148749/job/49009709966) is a repo in our organisation and shows and example of using this externally. This action can currently be run anywhere in the Cloudsmith github enterprise.
* In this repo the action has been run with both:
    * [job log extraction](https://github.com/cloudsmith-io/cloudsmith-ci-insights-action/actions/runs/17269395675)
    * [direct log indection](https://github.com/cloudsmith-io/cloudsmith-ci-insights-action/actions/runs/17269395698)

items.title: Feat: Add support for running benchmarks against existing Hazelcast clusters
items.body:
Currently, the Hazelcast Simulator assumes full control over the lifecycle of Hazelcast members by starting its own cluster during test execution. However, there are valid use cases, such as pre-provisioned or managed environments (IBM LinuxONE), where users need to run benchmarks against an existing Hazelcast cluster.

This pull request introduces functionality and new template that allows the simulator to connect to and execute benchmarks against a pre-existing Hazelcast cluster without launching its own members. This improves the flexibility and applicability of the simulator in more complex or enterprise-grade environments.

New Features: - Add a Kotlin DSL (`plugin`, `content`, `depends`, etc.) for succinct plugin test specification. - Extend the `LazyParser` API with incremental reparse support (`canBeReparsedIncrementally`) and a new `LazyLexingContext`. - Introduce project-opening flags (`PROJECT_OPENED_BY_PLATFORM_PROCESSOR`, `PROJECT_NEWLY_OPENED`, etc.) to better track how projects are opened by the platform. - Add a `DeduplicatingVirtualFileFilter` to prevent redundant VFS iterations in file indexing. - Expose undo manager feature flags (`isTransparentSupported`, `isConfirmationSupported`, `isGlobalSplitSupported`) for finer control. Bug Fixes: - Correct historical positional-only parameter error highlighting in Python. - Ensure `DeduplicatingVirtualFileFilter` respects delegate filters and prevents root access assertion failures. Enhancements: - Refactor the extension registry to support static and thread-local registries and provide dynamic swapping via `performWithExtensionSupport`/`buildExtensionSupport`. - Unify icon loading in `IconLoader` by consolidating implementations and removing deprecated paths. - Rewrite Python call-expression resolution and argument mapping in Kotlin with better handling of implicit/variadic parameters. - Improve Java code-block lazy parsing to use the new incremental API and remove deprecated cancellation hooks. - Allow configuring warning behavior in the Bazel JVM incremental builder via a new `--warn` flag. - Apply JUnit4 timeouts (`DisableOnDebug`) to numerous tests to improve reliability under load. - Update Bazel BUILD files to add required `//platform/core-ui` dependencies across many targets. CI: - Add `//platform/core-ui` dependency to many Bazel test targets to satisfy new UI APIs.

items.title: Add metadata for more modules and document metrics emitted
items.body:
Related to #13468 

Now that we can intercept metrics, I went through and found all the instrumentation modules that emit metrics by default and enabled the gathering on them. There are still many modules that emit metrics behind configuration flags, and I will followup with a strategy for how we can handle the case for telemetry gated by configuration options, and then enable the metric gathering on those remaining modules.

Also added more descriptions and configuration documentation, and refactored the collect script.

## Executive Summary: Authority Engine 4-Judge Protocol & CI Hardening

### Security Mechanisms
- Command-injection mitigations:
  - SelfHealingAgent adds validate_command(), safe_parse_command(), COMMAND_INJECTION_PATTERNS and JSON-extraction sanitization to reduce injection risk when generating/executing code.
- Credential handling and secrets:
  - ConfigManager reads LLM provider keys (ZAI_API_KEY, MISTRAL_API_KEY) from environment/.env and logs key access (names only). LLM calls use Bearer Authorization headers.
- Telemetry / tracing:
  - OpenLIT telemetry initialized (skipped in CI) and opik.track decorators instrument judge and authority flows.
- Missing/unchanged controls (not implemented in this PR):
  - No authentication or authorization on HTTP endpoints (/health, /webhook/hubspot, /execute) — endpoints are effectively public.
  - No rate-limiting or request-throttling middleware present.
  - No API key verification, OAuth, or CODEOWNERS-enforced runtime access controls.
  - No WAF, input-size limits, or hardened network policies introduced here.

### Database Schemas
- No database schema or ORM/model changes in this PR.
- Project moves toward stateless FastAPI services and filesystem-based workspace operations (FileSystemAgent usage in orchestrator).
- Redis is added to dependencies/metadata but no Redis data model, key schema, or usage patterns are implemented.

### External API Integrations
- HubSpot:
  - New async POST /webhook/hubspot endpoint (api/server.py) that accepts objectId and message_body and enqueues run_authority_flow via BackgroundTasks.
- LLM providers (4-Judge protocol):
  - RainmakerOrchestrator centralizes judge calls: prefers Z.ai when ZAI_API_KEY present, falls back to Mistral; uses JUDGE_MODELS role→model mapping.
  - HTTP calls made to provider chat/completions endpoints with Bearer tokens; responses parsed for JSON and used to drive authority/self-healing flows.
- Deployment / platform:
  - Vercel config changed to run Python/FastAPI entry (vercel.json) instead of Node/Next.js.
- Observability integrations:
  - prometheus-client added to dependencies (no instrumentation endpoints added in this PR).
- CI/tooling:
  - GitHub Actions workflow refactored to Python-first lint/test/build jobs; objectives mention branch protection, CodeRabbit, Lighthouse CI (these infra items are objectives but not yet fully implemented in repo files).

Summary verdict: The PR refactors the service to FastAPI with a new orchestrator, LLM-based 4-judge flows, command-safety helpers, and telemetry hooks while improving CI workflows. Critical operational security controls — endpoint authentication, rate limiting, and persistent datastore schemas — remain unaddressed and should be added before production rollout.
<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

items.title: Rewrite RPC
items.body:
## What's changed?

Rewrite RPC is used to support non-JVM languages for both parsing and recipe writing/running, allowing LSTs to be passed between process boundaries.

We had an initial implementation that we've worked on over the last year and which was called `rewrite-remote`. We're now incorporating the lessons we learned from that initial work to perfect(ish) the design as we bring it into openrewrite/rewrite.

We'll also be bringing rewrite-javascript/python/csharp into this repository as a part of this work, though maybe not all in this single PR.

@knutwannheden

items.title: Use endpoint as default connection option (ADR-119)
items.body:
This implements ADR-119[1], which specifies the client connection options to update requests to the endpoints implemented as part of ADR-042[2].

The endpoint may be one of the following:

a routing policy name (such as `main`)
a nonprod routing policy name (such as `nonprod:sandbox`)
a FQDN such as `foo.example.com`
The endpoint option is not valid with any of `environment`, `restHost` or `realtimeHost`, but we still intend to support the legacy options.

If the client has been configured to use any of these legacy options, then they should continue to work in the same way, using the same primary and fallback hostnames.

If the client has not been explicitly configured, then the hostnames will change to the new ably.net domain when the package is upgraded.

## Notes on implementation

As per previous guidance, I have _not_ updated tests to use the `endpoint` client option, but since the hostnames _are_ changing, then the assertions and default environment must be updated (from `sandbox` to `nonprod:sandbox`).

I have not added any explicit tests for the endpoint client option, but I can do this if we think it's necessary, although using `environment` is essentially the same as using `endpoint`.

See related Go and Ruby PRs:
* https://github.com/ably/ably-go/pull/679
* https://github.com/ably/ably-ruby/pull/441

[1] https://ably.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/ENG/pages/3428810778/ADR-119+ClientOptions+for+new+DNS+structure
[2] https://ably.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/ENG/pages/1791754276/ADR-042+DNS+Restructure

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **New Features**
  * Added an endpoint option to choose routing policies or provide a FQDN; default is "main". Added endpoint-driven hostname/fallback resolution.

* **Refactor**
  * Unified host resolution around endpoint; deprecated and removed direct rest_host/realtime_host/environment accessors in favor of endpoint, get_host/get_hosts and fallback_host.

* **Bug Fixes**
  * Improved hostname and fallback generation for production, nonprod, custom FQDNs, IPv4/IPv6, and localhost.

* **Tests**
  * Updated and added tests to validate endpoint behavior, renames, and expected URLs.

* **Documentation**
  * Docs updated to document endpoint usage and deprecations.

items.title: airbyte-ci: augment the report for java connectors

labels.name: CDK
labels.description: Connector Development Kit

items.body:
Today we're missing the logs (both JVM and container logs) in java connector reports.
This is creating a link to test artifacts. In the CI, the link will point to a zip file, while on a local run, it will point to a directory.

In addition, we recently added the junit XML inlined with the test standard output and error, but that didn't really work as well as we'd hoped: The reports were slow to load, they were not ordered by time, the corresponding logs were lacking. There's still a possibility they'll be useful, so rather than removing them altogether, they will be bundled in the log zip (or directory).

I'm also adding a button to copy the standard output or the standard error from a step into the clipboard.
Finally, I'm reducing the max vertical size of an expanded step, so it doesn't go over 70%, which seems much cleaner to me.

Here's an example of the result (from the child PR): https://storage.cloud.google.com/airbyte-ci-reports-multi/airbyte-ci/connectors/test/pull_request/stephane_02-09-add_background_thread_to_track_mssql_container_status/1708056420/d4683bfb7f90675c6b9e7c6d4bbad3f98c7a7550/source-mssql/3.7.0/output.html

items.body:
## Jira Reference
- Issue: TC-XXX
- Related GitHub Issue: #569

## Checklist
- [ ] Tests added/updated

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Executive Summary of Key Changes

### 1. Security mechanisms (rate limits, auth, hardening)
- New OIDC-based deployment/authentication for GitHub Actions → Alibaba Cloud (scripts/configure_alibaba_security.sh, workflows/type-safe-ci.yml, workflows/jules-governance.yml). CI uses an Alibaba RAM role with OIDC trust and scoped assume-role conditions to avoid long-lived secrets.
- Introduces a unified, type-safe SecurityConfig dataclass and loader (rainmaker_orchestrator/src/config/security.py: SecurityConfig, get_security_config) used by server startup. Includes:
  - Required env validation for ALIYUN_ACCESS_KEY_ID, ALIYUN_ACCESS_KEY_SECRET, ZAI_API_KEY, IFCLOUD_API_KEY.
  - sanitize_input() to strip dangerous characters.
  - redact_sensitive_info() to redact secrets for safe logging.
- Security hardening in CI and deployment:
  - Alibaba Cloud Access Analyzer integration to scan changed files and block merges/deploys on critical/high findings (security-analysis.yml, scripts/run_security_scan.js, .github/workflows/security-analysis.yml, .github/workflows/type-safe-ci.yml).
  - Reduced/explicit IAM scoping in role creation script (attaches Access Analyzer, ECS, VPC, and a custom ECS deploy policy).
  - PR governance/pr_analyzer.py plus Jules workflows enforce protected paths and a confidence-scoring gate (auto-blocks merges when protected paths changed or severity thresholds met).
- No explicit new rate-limiting middleware added to application code; security focus is on auth, secrets handling, CI-gated scans, and sanitization utilities.

### 2. Database schemas
- No schema or migration files changed. The change adds .env.example and other environment keys (database/Redis URL placeholders) but does not modify or introduce database table/ORM schema definitions.
- Summary: configuration and env scaffolding for DB/Redis added, but no DB schema updates in this PR.

### 3. External API integrations
- Alibaba Cloud:
  - Deep integration with Access Analyzer for automated security scanning, SARIF/report generation, and blocking logic (scripts/run_security_scan.js, src/integrations/alibabacloud.py, workflows/security-analysis/type-safe-ci).
  - Deployment via Alibaba ECS using OIDC-based role and Cloud Assistant in CI/CD pipelines (type-safe-ci.yml, configure_alibaba_security.sh).
- GLM / AutoGLM / Zhipu:
  - Multiple GLM-4.7 integration layers added (node and Python variants):
    - JS: src/integrations/zhipu-glm.js, src/orchestrators/autoglm.js (GLM integration + AutoGLM orchestration).
    - Python: src/integrations/zhipu_glm.py, src/orchestrators/autoglm.py, and endpoints (src/api/v1/endpoints/autoglm.py) exposing generation and autonomous security-analysis endpoints.
  - AutoGLM orchestrators combine GLM outputs with Alibaba findings to propose/simulate remediation and generate reports.
- Other platform integrations:
  - Expanded use of Grafana, Asana, Datadog, Ayrshare, and various AI provider SDKs across CI/workflows and server scaffolding (detailed in workflow and script changes).
  - New scripts and tooling to run security scans and produce artifacts/reports (scripts/run_security_scan.js, scripts/cloud_instance_audit.sh).
- Application-level API surface:
  - New FastAPI app and endpoints for GLM/AutoGLM (src/main.py, src/api/v1/endpoints/autoglm.py).
  - New JS/Node endpoints and server wiring (server.js and related JS modules) exposing GLM and AutoGLM features and health checks.

Overall impact: strong shift toward CI-gated, OIDC-authenticated deployments with automated security scanning (Alibaba Access Analyzer) and new autonomous GLM/AutoGLM integrations. No database schema changes; configuration and secret handling have been centralized and hardened, with sanitization and redaction utilities added.

items.title: Snowflake support ZIP-1190
items.body:
## Summary

1. Add EngineType.SNOWFLAKE to the api.thrift. 
2. Define a new SnowflakeImport stagingquery. The staging query takes a few additional parameters passed in as environment variables:
            "SNOWFLAKE_JDBC_URL"  - partial sf jdbc connection string, does not include the secret key which is to be retrieved runtime from SNOWFLAKE_VAULT_URI
            "SNOWFLAKE_VAULT_URI" - https://<vault_key_name>.vault.azure.net/secrets/<secret_name> azure key vault information
            "SNOWFLAKE_STORAGE_INTEGRATION" - name of the snowflake storage integration to be used to export data. This is either azure blob store, s3, gcs, etc. 

3. AzureKeyVaultHelper - azure specific implementation of retrieving vault secrets to complete the jdbc uri above.

The approach is to run a `COPY INTO` statement to snowflake that will export a table data into some location on azure blob store (or similar in other clouds). Then we will use spark to read in that parquet data and write it to an iceberg table. The teams.py has been modified to use polaris catalog as an example on azure, and in this case our polaris deployment is snowflake open catalog. 

## Checklist
- [ ] Added Unit Tests
- [ ] Covered by existing CI
- [ ] Integration tested
- [ ] Documentation update

items.title: Add GL upload function to Java interface
items.body:
(This builds upon https://github.com/KhronosGroup/KTX-Software/pull/957, so that should be merged first)

This adds bindings for the `ktxTexture_GLUpload` function to the Java interface.

There certainly are a few degrees of freedom about how _exactly_ that should be offered. And there is a trade-off between "trying to closely resemble the existing API" and "trying to create a nice API for Java". The main point here being that the function receives three _pointers_ (to `int` values, essentially). And pointers don't exist in Java.

A common (although not pretty) way is to emulate these with single-element arrays. So the call currently looks like this:
```
int texture[] = { 0 };
int target[] = { 0 };
int glError[] = { 0 };
int result = ktxTexture.glUpload(texture, target, glError);
```
This will fill the given arrays with the values that are returned from the native layer, accordingly.

Proper testing may be difficult. The basic call conditions are checked in a unit test. But beyond that, really _using_ the function will require an OpenGL context to be current, so that 1. requires an OpenGL binding for the test, and 2. can hardly happen during the standard test runs. 


---

I tried it out in a _very_ basic experiment. This experiment currently used https://www.lwjgl.org/ , but I'll probably also try it with https://jogamp.org/ . Given that LWJGL is the library behind https://libgdx.com/ and https://jmonkeyengine.org/ , that's likely to be a primary goal.

The result of that experiment is shown here...

![Khronos KTX in Java](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7c86d565-5fdf-4f31-a20c-f6616c2c99ed)

... and I frankly couldn't care less that it's upside down. It works 🙃
